parsed_findings.chain_steps: Attacker supplies `dir` in request args -> server builds f-string query with that value -> DB executes the resulting SQL string.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker may read arbitrary data, alter/erase tables, or bypass authorization depending on DB privileges.
parsed_findings.proof: Set `dir` to `asc;SELECT 1--` so the generated query contains attacker-controlled SQL after ORDER BY.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Invoke `list_users` with `request.args['dir']=asc;SELECT%201--` and observe the injected SQL becomes part of `q`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `dir` is interpolated into the SQL ORDER BY clause without validation, enabling SQL injection.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Attacker supplies internal URL->server fetches with axios->response is returned to attacker.
parsed_findings.impact: Can reach internal endpoints and exfiltrate data via `res.send(data.data)`.
parsed_findings.proof: `u=req.query.url||''` then `axios.get(u,{timeout:1500})` forwards `data.data` to the client.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: GET `/fetch?url=http://127.0.0.1:80/` and read the returned response body.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Unvalidated `url` query enables SSRF because it is passed to `axios.get` without allowlisting.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Whitelist check disabled->`bad target` not returned->`axios.get(u)` runs.
parsed_findings.impact: Allows arbitrary outbound fetches instead of restricting to the partner domain.
parsed_findings.proof: The `if (//api.partner.com'))` line contains `//` inside the condition, disabling the intended comparison logic.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: GET `/fetch?url=http://attacker.com/` and confirm it is not rejected with `400 'bad target'`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Intended partner-domain restriction is bypassed because the check is commented out.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Inject payload in note -> stored in DB -> later HTML rendering of note -> script execution in victim browser
parsed_findings.impact: Executed attacker JavaScript can steal session data or perform actions as the victim.
parsed_findings.proof: `inv.note=request.json.get('note',inv.note)` (line 6) is persisted via `db.save(inv)` (line 7) with no filtering.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send `PATCH /api/invoices/<inv_id>` with `{"note":"<script>alert(1)</script>"}` then open the UI page that displays the note.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `note` is saved without validation/sanitization, enabling stored XSS when rendered in a web page.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Craft `file` to bypass `../` stripping or use an absolute path; `path.join` resolves to attacker-chosen file; `fs.readFile` reads it; response returns content.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers may read sensitive files (e.g., credentials, keys) leading to data theft and further compromise.
parsed_findings.proof: Requesting `GET /download?file=/etc/passwd` should return `/etc/passwd` because `path.join('/srv/data',f)` can ignore the base for absolute paths.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send `GET /download?file=/etc/passwd` to `/download` and inspect the response body.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Untrusted `req.query.file` is weakly sanitized (only replaces one `../`), allowing path traversal/absolute-path bypass to read arbitrary files.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Controlled cookie `state` -> base64 decode -> `pickle.loads` deserializes attacker gadgets -> code execution.
parsed_findings.impact: Potential remote code execution in the application's process with its privileges.
parsed_findings.proof: Place a base64-encoded pickle gadget payload in the `state` cookie so unpickling triggers attacker-controlled code paths.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Set request cookie `state` to base64(malicious_pickle); send GET `/resume`; observe server-side payload execution.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Untrusted cookie data is base64-decoded and passed directly to `pickle.loads`, enabling malicious pickle deserialization.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: (1) Bypass/meet authorization in line 2 (e.g., self-update) (2) Send privileged properties in req.body (3) Object.assign writes them (4) db.users.save persists changes.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can escalate privileges or tamper with protected user attributes by overwriting fields that should be server-controlled.
parsed_findings.proof: Line 6 copies all properties from req.body into the loaded user object, and line 7 saves it without any field allowlist/validation.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Authenticate as any user allowed past line 2, then send PATCH with a body that sets a privileged field (e.g., {"isSupport":true}) and observe it saved.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The handler blindly persists attacker-controlled fields via Object.assign(user, req.body), enabling mass assignment of sensitive properties.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: (1) Authenticate as attacker (2) Supply victim user id in req.params.id (3) Pass the flawed/missing object-level check on line 2 (4) Load victim at line 5 (5) Overwrite via req.body and save.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker may modify other users’ records by exploiting insufficient object-level authorization.
parsed_findings.proof: Line 5 uses req.params.id to load a different user, while line 2’s access logic references req.user.* and does not reference req.params.id.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send PATCH /api/users/<victim_id> while authenticated as a user that can satisfy line 2, and include a changing field to update the victim.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The authorization check does not clearly tie the requester to the targeted :id, suggesting an IDOR where arbitrary users can be patched.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Attacker crafts `name`/`src` -> f-string builds `tar ...` command -> `os.system` invokes a shell -> injected metacharacters execute attacker commands.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can execute arbitrary shell commands on the server, potentially leading to full system compromise.
parsed_findings.proof: POST /backup with {"name":"x;id #","src":"/var/data/app"} makes cmd include `tar -czf /tmp/x;id #.tgz /var/data/app` which runs `id`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send POST `/backup` with JSON body using a `name` containing shell metacharacters (e.g., `;id #`).
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `name` and `src` are interpolated into a shell command executed via `os.system`, enabling OS command injection.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: POST stores attacker HTML in the `html` field; GET returns it; any frontend that inserts it into the DOM will execute it.
parsed_findings.impact: Stored XSS could steal sessions/CSRF tokens, deface pages, or execute actions as the victim user.
parsed_findings.proof: `body` from `request.json` is converted via `markdown.markdown(body)` and stored as `{'html': rendered}` with no escaping or filtering.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send POST `/comments` with `body` like ``, then GET `/comments` and render returned items in a browser.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled Markdown is rendered to raw HTML and stored without sanitization, enabling stored XSS when comments are later rendered.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Send negative `amount`; bypass `src < amt`; execute balance updates with inverted sign effects.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can mint funds and steal from arbitrary targets by using negative transfer amounts.
parsed_findings.proof: `amt = int(request.json['amount'])` is unchecked and `if src < amt` does not block `amt < 0`, so `src - amt` adds funds while `to` is updated with `+ amt` (effectively subtracting).
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `/transfer` with JSON `{"to":"target_id","amount":-10}` and any non-negative current balance; verify your balance increases and `target_id` decreases.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The endpoint allows negative `amount`, letting an attacker increase their own balance and decrease the `to` account.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Concurrent requests read the same `src`; both pass the insufficient-funds check; interleaved writes overwrite each other leading to wrong final balances.
parsed_findings.impact: Under concurrent use, funds accounting can become inconsistent, potentially overdrawing or otherwise corrupting balances.
parsed_findings.proof: The code performs a read (`db.get_balance(current_user.id)`) followed by multiple independent writes (`db.set_balance(...)`) without any transaction/locking shown.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Fire two concurrent POST `/transfer` requests that together exceed your balance (near the limit); both may pass `if src < amt` using the same initial `src`, then interleave writes to produce incorrect totals.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Non-atomic balance updates enable race conditions that can cause inconsistent balances and double-spend effects under concurrency.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1) Forge JWT with desired payload 2) Sign with wrong key 3) Make jwt.verify throw 4) Production catch returns jwt.decode 5) App treats decoded claims as valid.
parsed_findings.impact: Authentication/authorization can be bypassed because downstream code may trust the returned decoded claims (e.g., admin/role/userId).
parsed_findings.proof: jwt.verify(t, process.env.JWT_PUBLIC_KEY, ...) throws for invalid/tampered signatures; the catch branch for NODE_ENV==='production' returns jwt.decode(t) instead of failing.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Set NODE_ENV=production, then send Authorization: Bearer <JWT with correct format but wrong signature>; verifyToken returns decoded attacker-controlled claims.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: In production, verifyToken returns jwt.decode(t) on verification errors, which skips signature validation and can accept forged tokens.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use auth to satisfy `login_required`, pick `id=0` to pass the `if uid` gate, then read the returned `row`.
parsed_findings.impact: Potential disclosure of other users’ emails (IDOR) to any authenticated user.
parsed_findings.proof: The endpoint fetches `SELECT id,email FROM users WHERE id = %s` and returns `row` directly after only `login_required`, with no caller-to-row authorization.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: 1) Log in as any user 2) Request `GET /u?id=0` 3) If a user with id=0 exists, the response includes that user’s email.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Authenticated users can access user records via `/u?id=0` because the handler returns `db.fetch_one` results without any authorization/ownership check.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Timing oracle on `got == want` -> recover signature -> pass check -> call `process_event(json.loads(body))`.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker may bypass the signature check and trigger `process_event` with forged webhook data.
parsed_findings.proof: String equality on HMAC hex digests can leak per-character match timing, allowing an attacker to infer correct bytes over repeated requests.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send many requests to `/hook` with the same body and varying `X-Sig` guesses; measure response time and use timing to converge on the correct HMAC.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Webhook HMAC signature verification uses `got == want`, enabling an observable timing side-channel to gradually guess the correct signature.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Craft invalid/large JSON -> `json.loads(body)` throws -> no exception handling -> 500/slow response -> DoS.
parsed_findings.impact: Denial of service via repeated crashes or heavy parsing work; may also disrupt webhook processing.
parsed_findings.proof: `json.loads(body)` will raise on invalid JSON and the handler has no `try/except`, likely returning a 500 and consuming server resources on large inputs.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Post to `/hook` with a valid-looking `X-Sig` (or any signature that passes the check) and an invalid JSON body or an oversized body, then repeat to observe 500s/slowdowns.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Unvalidated request body is blindly parsed with `json.loads(body)` without size/format controls or error handling, allowing malformed/huge payloads to cause exceptions.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the open-redirect response as the first hop to lure a user into visiting an attacker domain.
parsed_findings.impact: Enables phishing and bypasses navigation-based trust controls by redirecting victims to attacker-controlled domains.
parsed_findings.proof: By requesting `/go?next=//evil.com`, `redirect(nxt)` emits a `Location: //evil.com` header which browsers interpret as an off-site redirect.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call `GET /go?next=//evil.com` and observe the browser navigate to `https://evil.com/` (scheme-relative redirect).
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The handler redirects to user-controlled `next` values that pass the `startswith('/')` check, allowing scheme-relative external redirects like `//evil.com`.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Craft encoded payloads to evade `next` validation and still reach an external scheme-relative redirect.
parsed_findings.impact: Makes the open redirect easier to reach because simple WAF checks that look for `//` in raw input may be evaded.
parsed_findings.proof: Requesting `/go?next=/%2Fevil.com` decodes to `//evil.com`, satisfying `nxt.startswith('/')` and reaching `redirect(nxt)`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call `GET /go?next=/%2Fevil.com` and verify the `Location` header becomes `//evil.com`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The slash-only validation can be bypassed using percent-encoding so `%2F` becomes `/`, turning an allowed path into a scheme-relative `//` destination.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Attacker controls request.data XML; lxml parses it with external entity resolution enabled; the external entity is expanded during etree.fromstring; the expanded entity content is returned via doc.findtext('title').
parsed_findings.impact: Potential sensitive file disclosure and/or SSRF from server-side XML entity resolution.
parsed_findings.proof: Send XML with a DOCTYPE external entity, e.g. <!DOCTYPE r [<!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd">]><root><title>&xxe;</title></root>.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST /xml/import with Content-Type application/xml and the XXE XML where &xxe; is placed inside <title>.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The endpoint enables XML External Entity processing (resolve_entities=True, no_network=False), allowing XXE that can read local files or trigger SSRF.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Send a login request with arbitrary credentials; the `if(true)` branch lets the server treat the user as logged in.
parsed_findings.impact: Authentication is bypassed, letting attackers access protected functionality without valid credentials.
parsed_findings.proof: `if ( true )` on line 4 makes the auth check always succeed regardless of credentials.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST to `/login` with any `email`/`password` values and observe the endpoint behaves as if credentials were valid.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The login result is effectively unconditional because the condition is `if (true)`, so any request can proceed as authenticated.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Craft request body to include query operators that make `findOne(q)` return an unintended document, then proceed based on the login control flow.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can manipulate the database query to authenticate as a victim or bypass credential checks.
parsed_findings.proof: `const q={email:req.body.email,password:req.body.password};` and `findOne(q)` pass raw input into the database query.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Provide an input like `email[$ne]=anything` (or equivalent query-operator payload) so the query matches unintended users.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `req.body` fields are used directly in the query object passed to `db.users.findOne(q)`, enabling NoSQL query/operator injection to alter auth logic.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Attacker supplies `url`->server performs `requests.get`->endpoint returns `len(data)` as side-channel about internal reachability/response size.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can reach internal network resources (blindly via returned `size`) and pivot to data access or actions if responses are processed further.
parsed_findings.proof: Line 3 reads `request.json['url']`, lines 4-6 only validate `http` prefix and call `requests.get(url,timeout=2)`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST /avatar/fetch with body `{"url":"http://127.0.0.1:80/"}` (or `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/`).
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `url` is only checked to start with `http` and is then fetched server-side, enabling SSRF to internal destinations.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Attacker supplies large `url`->server buffers full body into memory->resource consumption spikes->service degradation/outage.
parsed_findings.impact: Memory exhaustion or prolonged worker blocking can crash or make the service unavailable.
parsed_findings.proof: Line 6 uses `requests.get(...).content` which buffers the full response body; there is no max-bytes guard.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Set `url` to a large/streaming resource and repeat requests to exhaust RAM or worker capacity.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The code downloads the entire HTTP response into memory (`.content`) without any response-size limit, enabling DoS.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Overflow buf via gets -> overwrite saved return address/control data -> redirect execution to attacker-controlled location/ROP.
parsed_findings.impact: Potential denial of service and, with mitigations bypassed, arbitrary code execution via stack control-flow hijack.
parsed_findings.proof: Supplying >64 bytes (e.g., 80 'A's) overwrites nearby stack data/return address and can crash or redirect control flow.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Compile and run, then at `name:` paste a payload of 80+ bytes and observe a crash or hijacked behavior.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `gets(buf)` reads attacker-controlled input into a fixed 64-byte stack buffer without bounds checks.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: saveToken->SharedPreferences plaintext->local/backup/root access reads token->API calls as victim->account takeover
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can steal the access token and impersonate the user/API client, leading to account and data compromise.
parsed_findings.proof: saveToken() writes token to SharedPreferences('auth',MODE_PRIVATE) under key 'access_token' using putString(...).apply() with no encryption.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Trigger saveToken with a token, then extract the app's shared_prefs/auth.xml (or via backups/root) and read access_token.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Sensitive access token is stored in plain SharedPreferences without encryption, enabling token theft from local app storage.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Leak code/artifact -> extract KEY -> decrypt intercepted IV||CT -> recover plaintexts.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can decrypt all data protected by this function.
parsed_findings.proof: Line 2 sets KEY=b'0123456789abcdef' directly with no external secret management.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Get the deployed crypto_util.py (or binary/package), extract KEY, then decrypt captured ciphertexts (IV||CT).
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The AES key is hard-coded in source, enabling straightforward key recovery if code or build artifacts leak.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Intercept IV||CT -> craft modified ciphertext -> trigger distinct responses -> deduce plaintext or force controlled changes.
parsed_findings.impact: Attacker can modify plaintext or recover it under chosen-ciphertext conditions.
parsed_findings.proof: Lines 3-6 output iv+AES-CBC(pad(data,16)) with no MAC/HMAC/AEAD.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Flip bits or rearrange CBC blocks in transit; if any error/validation differences exist, infer or alter decrypted plaintext.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: AES-CBC encryption is returned without any integrity/authentication, allowing ciphertext tampering (and possible padding-oracle style attacks).

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Pass `out=NULL` with `out_sz>0` so the incorrect guard on line 3 fails, then reach `memcpy` on line 5.
parsed_findings.impact: Causes a denial-of-service crash (and potentially undefined behavior) when invalid pointers are passed.
parsed_findings.proof: If `out` is NULL and `out_sz>0`, the guard on line 3 does not return, then line 5 calls `memcpy(out, in, n)` and triggers a NULL pointer dereference.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call `copy_name("A", NULL, 8)`; execution reaches line 5 and dereferences `out==NULL`, crashing.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The NULL checks are incorrect: it returns -1 when `out` is non-NULL (`if (out || out_sz==0)`), so `memcpy(out, ...)` can dereference NULL when `out==NULL`.
